records.ety: [OE. shoven, AS. scofian, fr. scūfan; akin to OFries. skūva, D. schuiven, G. schieben, OHG. scioban, Icel. skūfa, skfa, Sw. skuffa, Dan. skuffe, Goth. afskiuban to put away, cast away; cf. Skr. kshubh to become agitated, to quake, Lith. skubrus quick, skubinti to hasten. √160. Cf. Sheaf a bundle of stalks, Scoop, Scuffle.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Shoved (shvd); p. pr. & vb. n. Shoving.]

defs: To drive along by the direct and continuous application of strength; to push; especially, to push (a body) so as to make it move along the surface of another body; as, to shove a boat on the water; to shove a table across the floor.
To push along, aside, or away, in a careless or rude manner; to jostle.
quotes: And shove away the worthy bidden guest.
He used to shove and elbow his fellow servants.

defs: To push or drive forward; to move onward by pushing or jostling.
To move off or along by an act pushing, as with an oar a pole used by one in a boat; sometimes with off.
quotes: He grasped the oar, eceived his guests on board, and shoved from shore.

defs: The act of shoving; a forcible push.
quotes: I rested . . . and then gave the boat another shove.
